attack_summary: A compromised Ubuntu host (192.168.1.3, agent rnxwtm) was running an active ICMP flood (hping3) targeting 7.0.14.254 (WAN). The adversary remained present and used the host to generate high-rate ICMP traffic (T1498.001).

mitigations.name: Inline Snort IPS insertion
mitigations.brief_description: Removed the direct FortiGate <-> switch link, added a preconfigured Snort IDS device, and placed it inline between FortiGate (WAN) and the Cisco switch (LAN). Verified reachability checks; Snort (inline IPS) blocked the outgoing ICMP flood signatures so legitimate connectivity was preserved while the flood was stopped.

LAN Connectivity Check (Ping).description: Checks inter-network connectivity by pinging a target host on the LAN. The target IP is defined by the fact 'lan.target.ip'.

Internet Connectivity Check (HTTP).description: Deadman ability that verifies internet connectivity by making an HTTP HEAD request to a well-known public endpoint. Uses curl instead of ping (ICMP) to test TCP-based network reachability. Returns the HTTP status code.

LAN Connectivity Check (Ping).description: Checks inter-network connectivity by pinging a target host on the LAN. The target IP is defined by the fact 'lan.target.ip'.

Internet Connectivity Check (HTTP).description: Deadman ability that verifies internet connectivity by making an HTTP HEAD request to a well-known public endpoint. Uses curl instead of ping (ICMP) to test TCP-based network reachability. Returns the HTTP status code.

mitigations.name: FortiGate egress deny policy to ICMP target
mitigations.brief_description: Created address objects for the compromised host and the ICMP target and added a FortiGate firewall policy denying PING from 192.168.1.3 to 7.0.14.254. (An attempted traffic-shaping parameter produced a parse error and was not applied.) The deny rule existed and blocked ICMP to the specified target, but placement/ordering and an overlapping permissive PING rule caused an unintended outage of some outbound connectivity (partial collateral impact noted).

LAN Connectivity Check (Ping).description: Checks inter-network connectivity by pinging a target host on the LAN. The target IP is defined by the fact 'lan.target.ip'.

Internet Connectivity Check (HTTP).description: Deadman ability that verifies internet connectivity by making an HTTP HEAD request to a well-known public endpoint. Uses curl instead of ping (ICMP) to test TCP-based network reachability. Returns the HTTP status code.

Internet Connectivity Check (HTTP).description: Deadman ability that verifies internet connectivity by making an HTTP HEAD request to a well-known public endpoint. Uses curl instead of ping (ICMP) to test TCP-based network reachability. Returns the HTTP status code.

mitigations.name: Host-level iptables egress rate-limit and drop
mitigations.brief_description: On the compromised Ubuntu host added an OUTPUT-chain iptables rule to ACCEPT ICMP limited to 5/sec with burst 10, then appended a DROP for remaining ICMP. Rule verified in iptables list; short low-rate tests succeeded while high-rate ICMP would be throttled/dropped at source.

key_observations: Layered (defense-in-depth) mitigations were applied: network IPS (Snort), firewall policy, and host egress controls — together they stopped the active ICMP flood.
All mitigations respected the constraint to keep the compromised host in-network and avoided quarantine/reimage.
Snort insertion was effective and preserved legitimate LAN/WAN reachability; firewall policy changes require careful ordering to avoid unintended service disruption.
Host-based throttling immediately reduced attack traffic at source and is a fast transferable technique, but it only covers the protocol(s) configured and requires persistence across reboots.
Verification outputs were sometimes inconclusive (beacon lines), so combining log inspection (Snort/FortiGate/host) with active tests is important to validate effects end-to-end.
overall_conclusion: The active ICMP flood was successfully stopped by applying layered mitigations: inline IPS insertion, targeted firewall deny policy, and host-level rate-limiting. The combined approach met the live-incident constraint (attacker remained present) and reduced attack success to zero. Recommended follow-ups: consolidate and persist host rules, review and tighten firewall policy ordering (remove or narrow permissive PING rules), enable/monitor IPS and firewall logs to confirm matches, apply broader egress filtering or rate-limiting for other protocols, and retain layered controls for resilience.
